title: Autoimmune Hepatitis
oneLiner: Chronic immune-mediated hepatocellular injury with interface hepatitis, elevated IgG, and autoantibodies; progresses to cirrhosis if untreated; responds to immunosuppression.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 32-year-old woman presents to the ED with two weeks of fatigue, jaundice, and right upper quadrant discomfort. She has no history of alcohol use, no recent travel, and her liver enzymes are markedly elevated — ALT 850 U/L, AST 620 U/L, ALP 180 U/L (mild rise), total bilirubin 120 µmol/L. Viral hepatitis serologies are negative. She has no fever. On exam, she has spider naevi, palmar erythema, and a smooth, tender hepatomegaly. Her IgG is elevated at 28 g/L, and anti-smooth muscle antibody (anti-actin) is positive at 1:320.

**Dissecting the Pattern Clues:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Young to middle-aged woman | Autoimmune diseases have female predominance (70-80%) |
| Insidious onset fatigue + jaundice | Not an acute viral or toxic hepatitis (which is more rapid) |
| Marked transaminase elevation (ALT/AST >> ALP) | Hepatocellular pattern, not cholestatic (ALP < 2× ULN) |
| Negative viral hepatitis serologies | Excludes HAV, HBV, HCV, HEV (common mimics) |
| Elevated IgG | Key hallmark: > 1.5× ULN supports diagnosis |
| Positive anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA) | Most specific for type 1 AIH (SLE also, but context matters) |
| Extrahepatic signs (spider naevi, palmar erythema) | Chronic liver disease; suggests advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis |
| No fever, no travel history | Rules out acute infectious hepatitis (e.g., leptospirosis, Q fever) |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Fulminant hepatitis:** Rapid onset encephalopathy, coagulopathy (INR > 1.5), jaundice within days. Can mimic paracetamol overdose or acute viral hepatitis. AIH accounts for ~5% of acute liver failure cases. Look for very high IgG and autoantibodies, but sometimes they are absent (seronegative AIH).
- **Asymptomatic with incidental abnormal LFTs:** A 55-year-old male with mildly elevated ALT (80–120 U/L) found on routine bloods. No symptoms, no jaundice. He may have anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) positive and normal IgG. This is easily missed and labelled as NAFLD. Liver biopsy often confirms AIH.
- **Cirrhosis presenting with variceal bleed:** A 60-year-old woman with no prior liver history presents with haematemesis. She has a cirrhotic liver on imaging, but you discover positive anti–liver kidney microsomal type 1 antibody (anti-LKM1). This is type 2 AIH, uncommon in adults but can present late with decompensated cirrhosis.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is a chronic liver disease where your immune system attacks your own liver cells, leading to inflammation and progressive scarring. It is not a specific infection, and it is not caused by alcohol or fatty liver. It is manageable with immunosuppression, but if left untreated, it silently destroys the liver.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Prevalence is ~20–30 per 100,000 in Europe/North America. Not rare — you will see 1–2 cases per year as an FY1.
- **Stakes:** Untreated AIH has a 5-year mortality of 50%. With treatment, 10-year survival > 80%. Early recognition prevents cirrhosis and decompensation.
- **Pitfalls:** It mimics viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury, and NAFLD. Missing the elevated IgG or autoantibodies leads to delayed diagnosis. Also, relapses are common after tapering steroids — don’t think it’s “cured.”

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**  
Autoimmune hepatitis is a T-cell–mediated attack on hepatocytes. Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4) plus a trigger (infection, drug, or unknown) breaks immune tolerance. Activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells recognise liver autoantigens (e.g., asialoglycoprotein receptor, cytochrome P450 2D6). This triggers:
- **Portal tract inflammation** with lymphocytes, plasma cells (hence IgG production).
- **Interface hepatitis** — inflammatory cells break through the limiting plate and invade the liver lobule.
- **Hepatocyte apoptosis and necrosis** → release of ALT, AST.
- **Fibrosis activation** by hepatic stellate cells → progressive scarring.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Sign / Finding | Mechanism |
|----------------|-----------|
| Elevated ALT/AST | Hepatocyte necrosis releases intracellular enzymes |
| Mildly elevated ALP | Adjacent bile ducts get secondary inflammation (but dominant injury is hepatocellular) |
| High IgG | Plasma cell infiltration in portal tracts; polyclonal B-cell activation |
| Positive autoantibodies (ASMA, ANA, anti-LKM1) | Circulating antibodies reflect loss of immune tolerance; often correlate with disease activity |
| Fatigue | Cytokine release (IL-6, TNF) — non-specific but dominant symptom |
| Jaundice | Impaired bilirubin conjugation + canalicular injury when inflammation is severe |
| Spider naevi / palmar erythema | Hyperoestrogenism due to impaired hepatic clearance of oestrogens (seen once cirrhosis develops) |
| Hepatomegaly (smooth) | Inflammation with oedema and cellular infiltration (later may become nodular if cirrhosis) |

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
The young woman with fatigue and markedly raised transaminases has active interface hepatitis. Her high IgG and ASMA reflect the plasma cell–rich infiltrate. Her spider naevi hint at already established fibrosis — AIH often smoulders for months before diagnosis.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific Cause / Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most common (80%) | No identifiable trigger: young woman with chronic hepatitis |
| **Drug-induced** | Minocycline, nitrofurantoin, statins, anti-TNF agents | Onset weeks to months after starting drug; history of acne (minocycline), recurrent UTIs (nitrofurantoin) |
| **Infectious** | EBV, CMV, hepatitis A/B/C, measles | Acute hepatitis with positive viral serologies; often self-limited, but can unmask AIH |
| **Autoimmune overlap** | Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) | Feature of both: e.g., high ALP + positive AMA (PBC) or cholangiography abnormal (PSC) |
| **Genetic** | HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4 | Family history of autoimmune disease (thyroid, coeliac) |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (unknown trigger) in a genetically susceptible individual.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Use the **Revised Original Score (1999)** or **Simplified Diagnostic Criteria (2008)**. The simplified version is easier for routine use: points for autoantibody titre, IgG level, histology, and exclusion of viral hepatitis.

*Simplified Score:*
| Parameter | Points |
|-----------|--------|
| ANA or ASMA ≥ 1:40 | 1 |
| ANA or ASMA ≥ 1:80 | 2 |
| Anti-LKM1 ≥ 1:40 | 2 |
| Anti–LC-1 (liver cytosol) positive | 2 |
| IgG > upper limit of normal | 1 |
| IgG > 1.1× upper limit of normal | 2 |
| Liver histology: interface hepatitis | 1 |
| Histology: plasma cell infiltrate | 1 |
| Exclusion of viral hepatitis | 1 |

- **≥6 points:** probable AIH; **≥7 points:** definite AIH.

**Key investigations with interpretation:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin, GGT)** | Hepatocellular injury vs cholestasis | Screen for liver injury | ALT/AST >> ALP (ratio > 3) suggests AIH; if ALP > 2× ULN, think overlap syndrome |
| **IgG** | Polyclonal elevation | Supports autoimmune aetiology; also used to monitor treatment response | > ULN (typically > 16 g/L) is 90% sensitive for AIH; > 1.1× ULN gives 2 points in diagnostic score |
| **Serum protein electrophoresis** | Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinaemia | Confirms IgG elevation pattern | Not necessary, but can be seen |
| **Autoantibody panel (ANA, ASMA, anti-LKM1, anti–LC-1)** | Specific antibodies | Type differentiation: type 1 = ASMA +/– ANA; type 2 = anti-LKM1 (rare in adults) | ANA is non-specific (also in SLE, drugs); ASMA > 1:160 strongly supports AIH; anti-LKM1 suggests type 2 (younger, more severe) |
| **Viral serology (HAV IgM, HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, HCV antibody, HEV IgM, EBV/CMV)** | Exclude acute viral hepatitis | Essential to rule out mimics before starting steroids | All negative in AIH |
| **Iron studies, copper studies** | Exclude haemochromatosis, Wilson's | Consider on first presentation especially in young adults | Transferrin saturation > 45% + ferritin high = haemochromatosis; caeruloplasmin low + Kayser-Fleischer rings = Wilson's |
| **Liver biopsy** | Histology: interface hepatitis, plasma cells, lobular inflammation, rosette formation | Gold standard for diagnosis; assesses fibrosis stage | Interface hepatitis + plasma cells = hallmark. Absence of steatohepatitis or viral inclusions helps differentiate |
| **Imaging (ultrasound liver + cirrhosis screen)** | Liver morphology, portal hypertension | Detect fibrosis, cirrhosis, HCC screening | Smooth liver early; nodular later; splenomegaly if portal hypertension |

**Pattern recognition – the single finding that confirms AIH:**  
**Positive anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA) at ≥ 1:80** in a patient with hepatocellular LFTs and hypergammaglobulinaemia (IgG elevated) is highly suggestive — > 80% specific for AIH if viral hepatitis is excluded. No single test is 100% diagnostic; histology remains the gold standard.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Stop all potential hepatotoxins:** Any drug that could be triggering (e.g., minocycline, nitrofurantoin). Also avoid NSAIDs and alcohol.
- **Evaluate severity:** Calculate MELD or Child-Pugh score. If INR > 1.5, bilirubin > 200 µmol/L, or encephalopathy — consider acute liver failure, escalate urgently.
- **Baseline labs:** LFTs, INR, IgG, autoantibodies, viral screen, pregnancy test (young women).

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Start immunosuppression** once diagnosis is confirmed (or strongly suspected with severe disease):
  - **First-line regimen (adults):** Prednisolone 30–60 mg oral daily (or IV if unable to take orally) for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 3–6 months.
  - **Plus** Azathioprine (AZA) as steroid-sparing: Start after 2 weeks of steroids (to assess response and check TPMT enzyme activity). Dose: 1–2 mg/kg oral daily.
- **Why this approach:** Steroids rapidly suppress interface hepatitis and reduce symptoms. Azathioprine allows lower steroid dose and reduces relapse risk. TPMT testing before AZA avoids severe bone marrow toxicity.
- **Monitor response:** Check LFTs every 1–2 weeks. Aim for ALT < 2× ULN within 6–8 weeks.

**Long-term:**
- **Maintenance therapy:** Azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day (or mycophenolate mofetil 500–1000 mg twice daily if intolerant) indefinitely. Low-dose prednisolone (5–10 mg/day) if needed.
- **Goal:** Normal LFTs or near-normal, asymptomatic. Re-biopsy if no improvement after 6–12 months (to rule out alternative diagnoses).
- **Surveillance:** If cirrhosis develops, screen for varices (OGD) and HCC (ultrasound + AFP every 6–12 months).
- **Lifestyle:** Avoid excess alcohol; ensure vitamin D and calcium (steroids ↑ osteoporosis risk); immunisations (flu, pneumococcal, Hepatitis A/B) before starting immunosuppression.

**Common pitfalls – what NOT to do:**
- **Do not start azathioprine first** without steroids in moderate–severe disease — it is too slow and may worsen disease transiently.
- **Do not use corticosteroids alone without considering azathioprine** for long-term — high relapse rate.
- **Do not assume normal LFTs means cure** — biopsy may still show interface hepatitis. Taper steroids too fast → rebound flare.
- **Do not forget to check TPMT** before azathioprine — risk of severe myelosuppression.
- **Do not miss pregnancy counselling** — AIH can flare postpartum; azathioprine is relatively safe but needs specialist input.

**Dose summary (inline):**
- Prednisolone 30–60 mg PO/IV daily for 2–4 weeks, reduce by 5–10 mg/week to maintenance 5–10 mg/day.
- Azathioprine 50–200 mg PO daily (1–2 mg/kg) based on TPMT activity.
- Mycophenolate mofetil 500–1000 mg PO twice daily (second-line for azathioprine failure/intolerance).

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Autoimmune Hepatitis | Drug-Induced Liver Injury | Acute Viral Hepatitis (HAV/HEV) | Primary Biliary Cholangitis |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Mild RUQ discomfort | May be absent | Epigastric/RUQ | Pruiritus early; dull RUQ pain |
| **Exam** | Jaundice, spider naevi, palmar erythema (if chronic) | Jaundice, no stigmata of chronic liver disease | Jaundice, mild hepatomegaly | Xanthelasma, hyperpigmentation, scratch marks (pruritus) |
| **LFT pattern** | ALT/AST >> ALP | ALT/AST >> ALP (hepatocellular) or mixed | ALT >> ALP (hepatocellular) | ALP >> ALT (cholestatic) |
| **IgG** | Elevated | Normal | Normal or slightly elevated (polyclonal) | IgM elevated (often > 2× ULN) |
| **Autoantibodies** | ASMA (+), ANA (+), anti-LKM1 (type 2) | None | None | AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibody) > 1:40 in 95% |
| **Key differentiating feature** | High IgG + ASMA; histology – interface hepatitis with plasma cells | Temporal relation to drug; positive rechallenge (if done) | Positive viral serology; self-limited | AMA + high ALP + pruritus; histology – florid bile duct lesions |
| **Treatment** | Immunosuppression | Stop drug; supportive care | Supportive care | Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) |

*Additional mimics:* Wilson disease (young, low caeruloplasmin, Kayser-Fleischer rings); Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (low AAT, emphysema); non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) (steatosis, insulin resistance).

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications of untreated or poorly controlled AIH:**

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Acute liver failure** (rare, ~5%) | Rapid jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy within days | INR > 1.5, ALT > 1000, bilirubin rising, confusion, asterixis | Immediate senior/ICU referral; consider N-acetylcysteine (controversial); liver transplant assessment |
| **Cirrhosis** (eventually develops in 30–50% of untreated) | Fatigue, ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy | Nodular liver on imaging, low platelets, INR prolonged, splenomegaly | Surveillance: 6-month US + AFP, OGD for varices. Treat complications (diuretics, beta-blockers, lactulose) |
| **Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)** | Pain, weight loss, rising AFP, liver mass | Associated with cirrhosis (annual risk ~1–2%) | Screening US + AFP every 6 months; treat with resection, ablation, or TACE if within Milan criteria |
| **Relapse after treatment withdrawal** | Flare of LFTs, symptoms return | ALT rises > 3× ULN, IgG increases | Reinstitute steroids ± azathioprine; taper slower |
| **Osteoporosis** (steroid side effect) | Fracture, back pain | Low BMD on DEXA | Calcium + vitamin D, bisphosphonates if needed |
| **Extrahepatic autoimmune disease** | Thyroiditis, coeliac, RA, SLE | Corresponding autoantibodies | Treat co-existing conditions separately |

**Late-presentation patterns:**
- A previously well 45-year-old man presents with **ascites and splenomegaly** and is found to have cirrhosis on US. LFTs are only mildly elevated. He has no history of alcohol. You discover he had slightly abnormal LFTs years ago but was lost to follow-up. Check IgG and ASMA — likely burnt-out AIH that progressed to cirrhosis.
- A 30-year-old woman with **recurrent miscarriages** and slightly high ALT – workup reveals positive ANA and ASMA with interface hepatitis on biopsy.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"AIH: A = Autoantibodies, I = IgG elevated, H = Hepatocellular injury (ALT >> ALP)"**
2. **"Type 1 AIH: SMOOTH muscle Ab – Most common, Older, Usually Hepatic"** (SMOOTH = S – smooth muscle Ab, M – most common, O – older (median age 40), O – often hepatic only, T – treatment with steroids, H – histology plasma cells)
3. **"Rules for steroids: Start high, Taper slow, Add AZA, Monitor closely"**

**Clinical Pearls (5–7 high-yield cues):**

1. **ALT >> ALP ratio > 5 is AIH until proven otherwise** in a young-to-middle-aged woman.
2. **Always check IgG** in every patient with unexplained hepatitis – a normal IgG makes AIH less likely (though ~10% have normal IgG).
3. **Anti-smooth muscle antibody is the star** – it’s more specific for AIH than ANA. An ASMA titre ≥ 1:80 in a patient with raised transaminases = AIH until proven not.
4. **Liver biopsy is still the gold standard** even with typical serology; it shows interface hepatitis + plasma cells and rules out steatohepatitis and viral inclusions.
5. **Never start azathioprine without knowing TPMT** – the most common error causing pancytopenia in FY1 slots.
6. **Relapse is common** (>50% within 2 years if steroids stopped too early). Don’t be fooled by normal LFTs; titrate to symptoms and IgG.
7. **Overlap syndromes** (~20%): If ALP is also raised, screen for AMA and anti-mitochondrial antibodies (PBC) or cholangiography (PSC). Treat with UDCA if PBC overlap.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Stopping steroids abruptly causing a steroid withdrawal flare (rebound hepatitis) – taper over months.
- Missing AIH in a patient with "just" mildly elevated ALT and normal IgG – always do autoantibodies.
- Forgetting to monitor bone density on long-term steroids – prescribe calcium/vitamin D.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Clinical Scenario | Action Threshold | Who to Contact |
|-------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Acute liver failure (ALF)** | Any: encephalopathy (any grade) + INR > 1.5 | Immediate ITU referral; hepatology on-call; consider transplant centre |
| **worse** | Rapidly rising ALT (e.g., doubles in 24 h) or bilirubin > 250 µmol/L | Senior medical registrar + hepatology |
| **Coagulopathy** | INR > 1.5 (not on warfarin) | Bleeding risk – discuss with haematology for vitamin K; escalate if INR > 2.0 |
| **Encephalopathy** | Any confusion, asterixis, reversed sleep cycle | Urgent ITU + hepatology; lactulose / rifaximin; exclude sepsis |
| **Neutropenia** (from azathioprine) | Neutrophils < 1.0 × 10⁹/L | Stop azathioprine; discuss risk of sepsis; consider G-CSF |
| **Severe sepsis** | NEWS2 ≥ 5 | Immediate sepsis 6; ICU if shock |
| **Hepatic decompensation** | New ascites (tense), variceal bleeding, jaundice with coagulopathy | Senior medical team; hepatology; consider TIPS if refractory |
| **Pregnancy related** | AIH flare post-partum (rapid ALT rise) | Urgent obstetric + hepatology review; restart steroids if not already on |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** → urgent physician review, consider ICU.
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** (not explained by hypoperfusion) → could indicate liver failure.
- **GCS < 15** → assess for hepatic encephalopathy; if < 8, protect airway.

**In summary:** AIH is a diagnosis made by pattern recognition – young woman, hepatocellular LFTs, raised IgG, positive ASMA. Management is immunosuppression with careful taper and lifelong monitoring. Miss it, and you condemn the patient to cirrhosis. Catch it early, and they can live a near-normal life.

## Autoimmune Hepatitis

**One-liner:** Chronic immune-mediated hepatocellular injury with interface hepatitis, elevated IgG, and autoantibodies; progresses to cirrhosis if untreated; responds to immunosuppression.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 32-year-old woman presents to the ED with two weeks of fatigue, jaundice, and right upper quadrant discomfort. She has no history of alcohol use, no recent travel, and her liver enzymes are markedly elevated — ALT 850 U/L, AST 620 U/L, ALP 180 U/L (mild rise), total bilirubin 120 µmol/L. Viral hepatitis serologies are negative. She has no fever. On exam, she has spider naevi, palmar erythema, and a smooth, tender hepatomegaly. Her IgG is elevated at 28 g/L, and anti-smooth muscle antibody (anti-actin) is positive at 1:320.

**Dissecting the Pattern Clues:**

| Clue | What It Tells You |
|------|-------------------|
| Young to middle-aged woman | Autoimmune diseases have female predominance (70-80%) |
| Insidious onset fatigue + jaundice | Not an acute viral or toxic hepatitis (which is more rapid) |
| Marked transaminase elevation (ALT/AST >> ALP) | Hepatocellular pattern, not cholestatic (ALP < 2× ULN) |
| Negative viral hepatitis serologies | Excludes HAV, HBV, HCV, HEV (common mimics) |
| Elevated IgG | Key hallmark: > 1.5× ULN supports diagnosis |
| Positive anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA) | Most specific for type 1 AIH (SLE also, but context matters) |
| Extrahepatic signs (spider naevi, palmar erythema) | Chronic liver disease; suggests advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis |
| No fever, no travel history | Rules out acute infectious hepatitis (e.g., leptospirosis, Q fever) |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Fulminant hepatitis:** Rapid onset encephalopathy, coagulopathy (INR > 1.5), jaundice within days. Can mimic paracetamol overdose or acute viral hepatitis. AIH accounts for ~5% of acute liver failure cases. Look for very high IgG and autoantibodies, but sometimes they are absent (seronegative AIH).
- **Asymptomatic with incidental abnormal LFTs:** A 55-year-old male with mildly elevated ALT (80–120 U/L) found on routine bloods. No symptoms, no jaundice. He may have anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) positive and normal IgG. This is easily missed and labelled as NAFLD. Liver biopsy often confirms AIH.
- **Cirrhosis presenting with variceal bleed:** A 60-year-old woman with no prior liver history presents with haematemesis. She has a cirrhotic liver on imaging, but you discover positive anti–liver kidney microsomal type 1 antibody (anti-LKM1). This is type 2 AIH, uncommon in adults but can present late with decompensated cirrhosis.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is a chronic liver disease where your immune system attacks your own liver cells, leading to inflammation and progressive scarring. It is not a specific infection, and it is not caused by alcohol or fatty liver. It is manageable with immunosuppression, but if left untreated, it silently destroys the liver.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Prevalence is ~20–30 per 100,000 in Europe/North America. Not rare — you will see 1–2 cases per year as an FY1.
- **Stakes:** Untreated AIH has a 5-year mortality of 50%. With treatment, 10-year survival > 80%. Early recognition prevents cirrhosis and decompensation.
- **Pitfalls:** It mimics viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury, and NAFLD. Missing the elevated IgG or autoantibodies leads to delayed diagnosis. Also, relapses are common after tapering steroids — don’t think it’s “cured.”

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
Autoimmune hepatitis is a T-cell–mediated attack on hepatocytes. Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4) plus a trigger (infection, drug, or unknown) breaks immune tolerance. Activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells recognise liver autoantigens (e.g., asialoglycoprotein receptor, cytochrome P450 2D6). This triggers:
- **Portal tract inflammation** with lymphocytes, plasma cells (hence IgG production).
- **Interface hepatitis** — inflammatory cells break through the limiting plate and invade the liver lobule.
- **Hepatocyte apoptosis and necrosis** → release of ALT, AST.
- **Fibrosis activation** by hepatic stellate cells → progressive scarring.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Sign / Finding | Mechanism |
|----------------|-----------|
| Elevated ALT/AST | Hepatocyte necrosis releases intracellular enzymes |
| Mildly elevated ALP | Adjacent bile ducts get secondary inflammation (but dominant injury is hepatocellular) |
| High IgG | Plasma cell infiltration in portal tracts; polyclonal B-cell activation |
| Positive autoantibodies (ASMA, ANA, anti-LKM1) | Circulating antibodies reflect loss of immune tolerance; often correlate with disease activity |
| Fatigue | Cytokine release (IL-6, TNF) — non-specific but dominant symptom |
| Jaundice | Impaired bilirubin conjugation + canalicular injury when inflammation is severe |
| Spider naevi / palmar erythema | Hyperoestrogenism due to impaired hepatic clearance of oestrogens (seen once cirrhosis develops) |
| Hepatomegaly (smooth) | Inflammation with oedema and cellular infiltration (later may become nodular if cirrhosis) |

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
The young woman with fatigue and markedly raised transaminases has active interface hepatitis. Her high IgG and ASMA reflect the plasma cell–rich infiltrate. Her spider naevi hint at already established fibrosis — AIH often smoulders for months before diagnosis.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Specific Cause / Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most common (80%) | No identifiable trigger: young woman with chronic hepatitis |
| **Drug-induced** | Minocycline, nitrofurantoin, statins, anti-TNF agents | Onset weeks to months after starting drug; history of acne (minocycline), recurrent UTIs (nitrofurantoin) |
| **Infectious** | EBV, CMV, hepatitis A/B/C, measles | Acute hepatitis with positive viral serologies; often self-limited, but can unmask AIH |
| **Autoimmune overlap** | Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) | Feature of both: e.g., high ALP + positive AMA (PBC) or cholangiography abnormal (PSC) |
| **Genetic** | HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4 | Family history of autoimmune disease (thyroid, coeliac) |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic (unknown trigger) in a genetically susceptible individual.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Use the **Revised Original Score (1999)** or **Simplified Diagnostic Criteria (2008)**. The simplified version is easier for routine use: points for autoantibody titre, IgG level, histology, and exclusion of viral hepatitis.

*Simplified Score:*
| Parameter | Points |
|-----------|--------|
| ANA or ASMA ≥ 1:40 | 1 |
| ANA or ASMA ≥ 1:80 | 2 |
| Anti-LKM1 ≥ 1:40 | 2 |
| Anti–LC-1 (liver cytosol) positive | 2 |
| IgG > upper limit of normal | 1 |
| IgG > 1.1× upper limit of normal | 2 |
| Liver histology: interface hepatitis | 1 |
| Histology: plasma cell infiltrate | 1 |
| Exclusion of viral hepatitis | 1 |

- **≥6 points:** probable AIH; **≥7 points:** definite AIH.

**Key investigations with interpretation:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin, GGT)** | Hepatocellular injury vs cholestasis | Screen for liver injury | ALT/AST >> ALP (ratio > 3) suggests AIH; if ALP > 2× ULN, think overlap syndrome |
| **IgG** | Polyclonal elevation | Supports autoimmune aetiology; also used to monitor treatment response | > ULN (typically > 16 g/L) is 90% sensitive for AIH; > 1.1× ULN gives 2 points in diagnostic score |
| **Serum protein electrophoresis** | Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinaemia | Confirms IgG elevation pattern | Not necessary, but can be seen |
| **Autoantibody panel (ANA, ASMA, anti-LKM1, anti–LC-1)** | Specific antibodies | Type differentiation: type 1 = ASMA +/– ANA; type 2 = anti-LKM1 (rare in adults) | ANA is non-specific (also in SLE, drugs); ASMA > 1:160 strongly supports AIH; anti-LKM1 suggests type 2 (younger, more severe) |
| **Viral serology (HAV IgM, HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, HCV antibody, HEV IgM, EBV/CMV)** | Exclude acute viral hepatitis | Essential to rule out mimics before starting steroids | All negative in AIH |
| **Iron studies, copper studies** | Exclude haemochromatosis, Wilson's | Consider on first presentation especially in young adults | Transferrin saturation > 45% + ferritin high = haemochromatosis; caeruloplasmin low + Kayser-Fleischer rings = Wilson's |
| **Liver biopsy** | Histology: interface hepatitis, plasma cells, lobular inflammation, rosette formation | Gold standard for diagnosis; assesses fibrosis stage | Interface hepatitis + plasma cells = hallmark. Absence of steatohepatitis or viral inclusions helps differentiate |
| **Imaging (ultrasound liver + cirrhosis screen)** | Liver morphology, portal hypertension | Detect fibrosis, cirrhosis, HCC screening | Smooth liver early; nodular later; splenomegaly if portal hypertension |

**Pattern recognition – the single finding that confirms AIH:**  
**Positive anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA) at ≥ 1:80** in a patient with hepatocellular LFTs and hypergammaglobulinaemia (IgG elevated) is highly suggestive — > 80% specific for AIH if viral hepatitis is excluded. No single test is 100% diagnostic; histology remains the gold standard.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Stop all potential hepatotoxins:** Any drug that could be triggering (e.g., minocycline, nitrofurantoin). Also avoid NSAIDs and alcohol.
- **Evaluate severity:** Calculate MELD or Child-Pugh score. If INR > 1.5, bilirubin > 200 µmol/L, or encephalopathy — consider acute liver failure, escalate urgently.
- **Baseline labs:** LFTs, INR, IgG, autoantibodies, viral screen, pregnancy test (young women).

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Start immunosuppression** once diagnosis is confirmed (or strongly suspected with severe disease):
  - **First-line regimen (adults):** Prednisolone 30–60 mg oral daily (or IV if unable to take orally) for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 3–6 months.
  - **Plus** Azathioprine (AZA) as steroid-sparing: Start after 2 weeks of steroids (to assess response and check TPMT enzyme activity). Dose: 1–2 mg/kg oral daily.
- **Why this approach:** Steroids rapidly suppress interface hepatitis and reduce symptoms. Azathioprine allows lower steroid dose and reduces relapse risk. TPMT testing before AZA avoids severe bone marrow toxicity.
- **Monitor response:** Check LFTs every 1–2 weeks. Aim for ALT < 2× ULN within 6–8 weeks.

**Long-term:**
- **Maintenance therapy:** Azathioprine 1–2 mg/kg/day (or mycophenolate mofetil 500–1000 mg twice daily if intolerant) indefinitely. Low-dose prednisolone (5–10 mg/day) if needed.
- **Goal:** Normal LFTs or near-normal, asymptomatic. Re-biopsy if no improvement after 6–12 months (to rule out alternative diagnoses).
- **Surveillance:** If cirrhosis develops, screen for varices (OGD) and HCC (ultrasound + AFP every 6–12 months).
- **Lifestyle:** Avoid excess alcohol; ensure vitamin D and calcium (steroids ↑ osteoporosis risk); immunisations (flu, pneumococcal, Hepatitis A/B) before starting immunosuppression.

**Common pitfalls – what NOT to do:**
- **Do not start azathioprine first** without steroids in moderate–severe disease — it is too slow and may worsen disease transiently.
- **Do not use corticosteroids alone without considering azathioprine** for long-term — high relapse rate.
- **Do not assume normal LFTs means cure** — biopsy may still show interface hepatitis. Taper steroids too fast → rebound flare.
- **Do not forget to check TPMT** before azathioprine — risk of severe myelosuppression.
- **Do not miss pregnancy counselling** — AIH can flare postpartum; azathioprine is relatively safe but needs specialist input.

**Dose summary (inline):**
- Prednisolone 30–60 mg PO/IV daily for 2–4 weeks, reduce by 5–10 mg/week to maintenance 5–10 mg/day.
- Azathioprine 50–200 mg PO daily (1–2 mg/kg) based on TPMT activity.
- Mycophenolate mofetil 500–1000 mg PO twice daily (second-line for azathioprine failure/intolerance).

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Autoimmune Hepatitis | Drug-Induced Liver Injury | Acute Viral Hepatitis (HAV/HEV) | Primary Biliary Cholangitis |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Mild RUQ discomfort | May be absent | Epigastric/RUQ | Pruiritus early; dull RUQ pain |
| **Exam** | Jaundice, spider naevi, palmar erythema (if chronic) | Jaundice, no stigmata of chronic liver disease | Jaundice, mild hepatomegaly | Xanthelasma, hyperpigmentation, scratch marks (pruritus) |
| **LFT pattern** | ALT/AST >> ALP | ALT/AST >> ALP (hepatocellular) or mixed | ALT >> ALP (hepatocellular) | ALP >> ALT (cholestatic) |
| **IgG** | Elevated | Normal | Normal or slightly elevated (polyclonal) | IgM elevated (often > 2× ULN) |
| **Autoantibodies** | ASMA (+), ANA (+), anti-LKM1 (type 2) | None | None | AMA (anti-mitochondrial antibody) > 1:40 in 95% |
| **Key differentiating feature** | High IgG + ASMA; histology – interface hepatitis with plasma cells | Temporal relation to drug; positive rechallenge (if done) | Positive viral serology; self-limited | AMA + high ALP + pruritus; histology – florid bile duct lesions |
| **Treatment** | Immunosuppression | Stop drug; supportive care | Supportive care | Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) |

*Additional mimics:* Wilson disease (young, low caeruloplasmin, Kayser-Fleischer rings); Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (low AAT, emphysema); non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) (steatosis, insulin resistance).

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications of untreated or poorly controlled AIH:**

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Acute liver failure** (rare, ~5%) | Rapid jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy within days | INR > 1.5, ALT > 1000, bilirubin rising, confusion, asterixis | Immediate senior/ICU referral; consider N-acetylcysteine (controversial); liver transplant assessment |
| **Cirrhosis** (eventually develops in 30–50% of untreated) | Fatigue, ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy | Nodular liver on imaging, low platelets, INR prolonged, splenomegaly | Surveillance: 6-month US + AFP, OGD for varices. Treat complications (diuretics, beta-blockers, lactulose) |
| **Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)** | Pain, weight loss, rising AFP, liver mass | Associated with cirrhosis (annual risk ~1–2%) | Screening US + AFP every 6 months; treat with resection, ablation, or TACE if within Milan criteria |
| **Relapse after treatment withdrawal** | Flare of LFTs, symptoms return | ALT rises > 3× ULN, IgG increases | Reinstitute steroids ± azathioprine; taper slower |
| **Osteoporosis** (steroid side effect) | Fracture, back pain | Low BMD on DEXA | Calcium + vitamin D, bisphosphonates if needed |
| **Extrahepatic autoimmune disease** | Thyroiditis, coeliac, RA, SLE | Corresponding autoantibodies | Treat co-existing conditions separately |

**Late-presentation patterns:**
- A previously well 45-year-old man presents with **ascites and splenomegaly** and is found to have cirrhosis on US. LFTs are only mildly elevated. He has no history of alcohol. You discover he had slightly abnormal LFTs years ago but was lost to follow-up. Check IgG and ASMA — likely burnt-out AIH that progressed to cirrhosis.
- A 30-year-old woman with **recurrent miscarriages** and slightly high ALT – workup reveals positive ANA and ASMA with interface hepatitis on biopsy.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **"AIH: A = Autoantibodies, I = IgG elevated, H = Hepatocellular injury (ALT >> ALP)"**
2. **"Type 1 AIH: SMOOTH muscle Ab – Most common, Older, Usually Hepatic"** (SMOOTH = S – smooth muscle Ab, M – most common, O – older (median age 40), O – often hepatic only, T – treatment with steroids, H – histology plasma cells)
3. **"Rules for steroids: Start high, Taper slow, Add AZA, Monitor closely"**

**Clinical Pearls (5–7 high-yield cues):**

1. **ALT >> ALP ratio > 5 is AIH until proven otherwise** in a young-to-middle-aged woman.
2. **Always check IgG** in every patient with unexplained hepatitis – a normal IgG makes AIH less likely (though ~10% have normal IgG).
3. **Anti-smooth muscle antibody is the star** – it’s more specific for AIH than ANA. An ASMA titre ≥ 1:80 in a patient with raised transaminases = AIH until proven not.
4. **Liver biopsy is still the gold standard** even with typical serology; it shows interface hepatitis + plasma cells and rules out steatohepatitis and viral inclusions.
5. **Never start azathioprine without knowing TPMT** – the most common error causing pancytopenia in FY1 slots.
6. **Relapse is common** (>50% within 2 years if steroids stopped too early). Don’t be fooled by normal LFTs; titrate to symptoms and IgG.
7. **Overlap syndromes** (~20%): If ALP is also raised, screen for AMA and anti-mitochondrial antibodies (PBC) or cholangiography (PSC). Treat with UDCA if PBC overlap.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Stopping steroids abruptly causing a steroid withdrawal flare (rebound hepatitis) – taper over months.
- Missing AIH in a patient with "just" mildly elevated ALT and normal IgG – always do autoantibodies.
- Forgetting to monitor bone density on long-term steroids – prescribe calcium/vitamin D.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Clinical Scenario | Action Threshold | Who to Contact |
|-------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Acute liver failure (ALF)** | Any: encephalopathy (any grade) + INR > 1.5 | Immediate ITU referral; hepatology on-call; consider transplant centre |
| **worse** | Rapidly rising ALT (e.g., doubles in 24 h) or bilirubin > 250 µmol/L | Senior medical registrar + hepatology |
| **Coagulopathy** | INR > 1.5 (not on warfarin) | Bleeding risk – discuss with haematology for vitamin K; escalate if INR > 2.0 |
| **Encephalopathy** | Any confusion, asterixis, reversed sleep cycle | Urgent ITU + hepatology; lactulose / rifaximin; exclude sepsis |
| **Neutropenia** (from azathioprine) | Neutrophils < 1.0 × 10⁹/L | Stop azathioprine; discuss risk of sepsis; consider G-CSF |
| **Severe sepsis** | NEWS2 ≥ 5 | Immediate sepsis 6; ICU if shock |
| **Hepatic decompensation** | New ascites (tense), variceal bleeding, jaundice with coagulopathy | Senior medical team; hepatology; consider TIPS if refractory |
| **Pregnancy related** | AIH flare post-partum (rapid ALT rise) | Urgent obstetric + hepatology review; restart steroids if not already on |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** → urgent physician review, consider ICU.
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** (not explained by hypoperfusion) → could indicate liver failure.
- **GCS < 15** → assess for hepatic encephalopathy; if < 8, protect airway.

**In summary:** AIH is a diagnosis made by pattern recognition – young woman, hepatocellular LFTs, raised IgG, positive ASMA. Management is immunosuppression with careful taper and lifelong monitoring. Miss it, and you condemn the patient to cirrhosis. Catch it early, and they can live a near-normal life.
